text: Robotic lumbar hernia repair with mesh. Traditionally, due to the location of lumbar hernias, repair occurs via an open technique. In this video, we will discuss a robotic preperitoneal technique for repair of a lumbar hernia.

The patient is a seventy-eight year old female who presented to clinic with a seven to eight months of right lower back bulge and pain. Of note, she had suffered from these symptoms before. In two thousand seven, she had had her right lumbar hernia repaired via an open technique with mesh. One year later, in two thousand eight, she suffered recurrence and again had an open repair of her lumbar hernia with mesh.

As a part of the diagnostic workup, we obtained a CT abdomen pelvis which showed a right lumbar hernia containing retroperitoneal and perineptic fat. After discussion and case conference, we decided the best operation for this patient would be a robotic preperitoneal hernia repair with mesh and a fibrin seal.

The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position, and the abdomen is accessed with a Veress needle. The abdomen is insufflated to 15 mmHg, and two additional working ports are placed under direct visualization in the right abdomen for exploration and dissection.

The da Vinci robot is docked, and the camera is inserted through an 8mm mid-abdominal port. Inside the abdomen, the surgeon begins retroperitoneal dissection using blunt and sharp dissection techniques along with electrocautery. The peritoneum is entered near the liver, and dissection proceeds laterally until the hernia sac and retroperitoneal fat are identified. The previous mesh is visible inferiorly and posteriorly relative to the hernia sac.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to dissect around the hernia sac, with the cursor indicating the location of the previous mesh. The previous mesh is carefully removed to facilitate the reduction of the hernia sac. Following the removal, the dissection continues, and the contents of the hernia sac are fully reduced into the abdomen.

The surgeon closes the hernia defect using a running O stratific suture, reinforcing the closure with interrupted PDS sutures. After achieving primary closure, the defect is measured to determine the appropriate size for the mesh.

The surgeon excises the mesh from the immobilized peritoneum and places it aside for removal. A 12x12 piece of mesh with rounded corners is introduced and aligned to cover the defect. The superior edge of the mesh is fixated using a running Stratifix suture. The excised mesh is removed through a port site using an endo-catch bag, and fascial stitches are positioned for later closure.

The camera view shows the mesh fixated on the superior edge, covering the primarily closed hernia defect. Fiber and glue are applied over the mesh to secure it to the abdominal wall. The flap is closed using a running O stratifix suture.
The surgeon inspects the abdomen thoroughly to ensure hemostasis. Laparoscopic ports are removed, and the fascia is closed, marking the end of the procedure.

The purpose of this video is to present a minimally invasive approach to recurrent lumbar hernia repair, specifically a robotic preperitoneal approach, as a promising alternative to open repair of lumbar hernias.
